coredata.dc:title: Crosslinking induced photoluminescence quenching in polyvinyl alcohol-carbon quantum dot composite
coredata.dc:creator: Hoang, Q.-B.
Mai, V.-T.
Nguyen, D.-K.
Truong, D.Q.
Mai, X.-D.
coredata.dc:description: Highly luminescent and water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been integrated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) recently to realize composites with synergistic functionalities. The effects of chemical interactions between CQDs and PVA matrix on the changes in optical properties of CQDs or physicochemical properties of PVA are yet to be investigated fully. Herein, we first synthesized CQDs with different surface chemistry via varying the molar ratio between citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine and then embedded the CQDs into PVA with or without additional CA crosslinking. Infrared and UV–vis studies on CQDs and composites showed that condensation between CQDs and PVA induced a redshift in the absorption peak originating from CQDs’ surface fluorophore groups. Interestingly, CA crosslinking significantly increased the water resistance, but it quenched the photoluminescence of composites. The quenching effects were reduced by using CQDs having less reactivity with CA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface fluorophore
Chemical interactions
Polymer matrix
Surface acidity
Optical changes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis and purification of CQDs
The fabrication of CQD-PVA composite membrane
Testing the water resistance of composite membranes
Structure and optical properties of CQDs
The optical properties of CQD-PVA composite
The photoluminescence quenching in composite by CA crosslinking

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Vietnam Ministry of Education and Training

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Hanoi Pedagogical University 2 (HPU2) under Grant no. C.2017-18-05 and partially funded by Vietnam Ministry of Education and Training via the Research and Development Foundation of HPU2 under Grant no B.2018-SP2-13 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of N-CQDs.TEM, transmission electron microscopy; CQDs, carbon quantum dots.
a) XPS survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s spectra of N-CQDs.XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy; CQDs, carbon quantum dots.

Optical properties of N-CQDs. (a) absorption spectra of solution with varying concentration; (b) absorption, photoexcitation (PLE), and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of N-CQDs solution (5 mg/ml); (c) the dependence of PL spectra on excitation wavelength; (d) the determination of PL quantum yield.CQDs, carbon quantum dots.

Properties of N-CQD-PVA composite. (a) FT-IR spectra of CA, PVA, and N-CQDs and their composite membranes. In (a), the plots were vertically shifted for clarify. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of solution (s) and membrane (m) states; c) the absorption (UV–vis), photoexcitation (PLE), and excitation (λex)-dependent PL spectra and (d) UV stabilities of N-CQD-PVA composite membranes. Inset in (d) shows the composite membrane under UV lamp (360 nm). The effects of N-CQDs and CA on the water-solubility (e) and thermal (f) resistances of PVA composites.CQDs, carbon quantum dots; PVA, polyvinyl alcohol; CA, citric acid; PL, photoluminescence; Comp, N-CQD-PVA.

a) FT-IR spectra of CQDs synthesized from CA and EDA with –COOH/–NH2 molar ratio of 2.3/1 (A-CQDs), 1/1 (N-CQDs), and 1/2.3 (B-CQDs). The spectra were vertically shifted for clarify. (b) PL spectra of composite membranes having the same optical density at excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Dashed and solid lines present composite with or without additional CA crosslinking, respectively. (c) Integrated PL deduced from (b) for composite with (patterned) or without (filled) CA crosslinking.CQDs, carbon quantum dots; CA, citric acid; EDA, ethylenediamine; PL, photoluminescence; FT-IR, Fourier transform infrared.
Crosslinking induced PL quenching mechanism.PL, photoluminescence.

head.ce:title: Crosslinking induced photoluminescence quenching in polyvinyl alcohol-carbon quantum dot composite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Research and Development, Duy Tan University, 3 Quang Trung, Da Nang, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Hanoi Pedagogical University 2, 32 Nguyen Van Linh, Phuc Yen, Vinh Phuc, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering Physics, Hanoi University of Science and Technology, 1 Dai Co Viet, Hanoi, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hanoi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Natural Science, Electric Power University, 235 Hoang Quoc Viet, Hanoi, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Multidisciplinary Research for Advanced Materials, Tohoku University, Sendai 980-8577, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Multidisciplinary Research for Advanced Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Hanoi Pedagogical University 2, 32 Nguyen Van Linh, Phuc Yen, Vinh Phuc, Viet Nam.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly luminescent and water-soluble carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been integrated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) recently to realize composites with synergistic functionalities. The effects of chemical interactions between CQDs and PVA matrix on the changes in optical properties of CQDs or physicochemical properties of PVA are yet to be investigated fully. Herein, we first synthesized CQDs with different surface chemistry via varying the molar ratio between citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine and then embedded the CQDs into PVA with or without additional CA crosslinking. Infrared and UV–vis studies on CQDs and composites showed that condensation between CQDs and PVA induced a redshift in the absorption peak originating from CQDs’ surface fluorophore groups. Interestingly, CA crosslinking significantly increased the water resistance, but it quenched the photoluminescence of composites. The quenching effects were reduced by using CQDs having less reactivity with CA.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the first report by Xu et al. [1], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged to be potential alternatives to visible emitting quantum dots (QDs) (CdSe, InP, etc) because of their distinct merits of chemical abundance, facile color tunability [2,3], high emission quantum yields [4], and excellent biocompatibility [5]. Thus far, solvothermal syntheses are preferred to prepare CQDs from many different carbon resources. Under mild solvothermal conditions (c.a. 200°C), simple organic molecules, such as citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) undergo condensation, polymerization, and carbonization processes to form CQDs [6,7]. Recent spectroscopic studies suggested that CQDs derived from CA contain at least three components namely carbon core involving polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) of different sizes, surface molecular-like fluorophore, and simple surface functional groups, e.g. –OH, –NH–, –COOH [7–11]. While the surface groups determine the solubility, PAHs and fluorophore account vastly for the optical properties of CQDs.

Embedding QDs into polymer matrix is a convenient approach to protect QDs from working environment and to explore new functionalities of resultant composites [12–14]. Recently, combining CQDs and polymers to form composites that have unique functionalities has been demonstrated. Kumar et al. introduced nitrogen-doped CQDs into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to tune the refractive index of CQD-PVA composite from c.a. 1.55 to 1.90 at 700 nm [15]. The Wu et al. and Yang et al. used CQDs to cross-link PVA chains within CQD-PVA composites that showed shape-memory effects [16,17]. The combination of CQDs having low lying states and insulating PVA could give rise to charge trapping ability of resultant composites as demonstrated by Ambasankar et al. [18]. These new functionalities and the well-known light-converting ability [2,19–21] make CQD-polymer composites to be today materials for diverse applications. It is worthy to note that PVA is preferably used for making homogeneous CQD-polymer composites because both hydrothermally synthesized CQDs and PVA are highly water soluble. However, there is a concern about the humid stability of CQD-PVA composites as they readily absorb water. Though crosslinking PVA is necessary to increase water resistance of PVA-based composites [22], the effects of crosslinking on the optical properties of CQD-PVA composites are not explored yet. Herein, we compared the emission properties of CQD-PVA composites with or without crosslinking and revealed that CA crosslinking induced photoluminescence (PL) quenching. The quenching effects decreased in the composite where CQDs having more acidic surfaces were used.

CA monohydrate (99.8%, Aladdin Chemicals), EDA (99.5%, Aladdin Chemicals), PVA (Mw∼67000, Aladdin Chemicals), quinine sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich), and sulfuric acid (97%, Aladdin Chemicals) were purchased and used directly without any purifications. Dialysis bags with a molecular weight cut-off of 2000 kDa were purchased from Scientific Research Special. 50 ml hydrothermal reactors having polyphenylene (PPL) line-in were supplied by Tansuozhe Company.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and purification of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 35 ml of aqueous solution was prepared in a PPL hydrothermal cup by mixing 5 M mother solutions of CA and EDA. The volumes of CA and EDA solutions were varied so that –COOH/–NH2 molar ratio was 2.3/1, 1/1, or 1/2.3. The mixture solution was bubbled with N2 gas for 10 min to remove dissolved oxygen. The PPL cup was then closed, put into a stainless steel shell, and transferred to a temperature-controlled oven, where the reaction was carried out at 200°C for 6 h. For purification of CQDs, after being cooled naturally to room temperature, acetone was added into resultant solution until a black precipitate appeared. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 7000 rpm for 5 min and re-dissolved in water. The solvent-antisolvent purification process was repeated three times to get CQDs solution. To remove large aggregations, the solution was filtered by using syringe filters having a pore size of 0.21 μm. Finally, the CQD solution was dialyzed against deionized (DI) water to remove small products, which contained mainly molecular-like fluorophore [23]. We denoted A-CQDs, N-CQDs, and B-CQDs for samples that were obtained when the –COOH/–NH2 molar ratio was 2.3/1, 1/1, or 1/2.3, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fabrication of CQD-PVA composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g of PVA was dissolved in 70 ml of DI water at 70°C before adding a CQDs solution which contained 50 mg of CQDs. For crosslinking PVA with CA, an aqueous solution containing 55 mg of CA was added to the above mixture. The mixture solution was stirred at about 50°C to evaporate water until total solids reached 7.5% by mass. The mixture was then centrifuged to eliminate air bubbles, poured onto a glass substrate, and flattened by a glass rod. To control the thickness of composite membranes, the glass rod was guided by 2 mm-thick stainless steel barriers. The composite membranes were peeled from glass substrate after being dried at 60°C for 12h. The membranes were sequentially dried at 100°C for 1 h and at 130°C for 2 h. Finally, the dried membranes were stored in a vacuum box for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing the water resistance of composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the water resistance of composite, we monitored the remaining dried weight of composite that was immersed in water for different periods of time. Briefly, dried membranes including PVA, N-CQD-PVA (denoted as Comp) and CA-crosslinked Comp (denoted as Comp + CA) with a thickness of about 0.5 mm were cut into 1 × 10 mm slices. 1 g of slices were packed into a cotton bag and immersed into stirred water bath at 40°C. After different periods of time, the bag was weighted following by being dried 130°C for 1 h.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs solutions and composite membranes were conducted on an UV-2450 (Shimadzu) spectrometer. PL spectra were carried out on a Nanolog spectrometer which involves a Xe lamp as excitation light source and a charge coupled device (CCD) detector. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Jasco FT/IR6300 spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CQDs were obtained on JEM 2100 microscope operating at 200 kV using LaB6 filament. Chemical composition of CQDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) conducted on an ULVAC PHI 500 (Versa Probe II) equipped with a Al Kα X-ray source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows representative TEM images of purified N-CQDs. CQD assembly consisted of particles with different degrees of carbon density as indicated by varying dark contrast among particles. The QDs size ranged from 3 to 8 nm. We did observe CQDs which shows lattice spacing of 0.33 nm corresponding to (002) plane of graphite structure [21]. Based on the mechanism described previously, CQDs were formed from multiple processes such as polymerization, condensation, and hydrothermal carbonization [6,11]. Increasing reaction temperature or reaction time improves the carbonization process. As the result, CQDs were produced with richer PAH content [7,11]. After a given period of reaction time, a mixture of CQDs with different sizes and PAH contents was usually obtained [23] as what we see in Fig. 1.

XPS survey spectrum shown in Fig. 2 a indicated CQDs containing O, C, and N elements. Carbon presented mostly in the form of C sp2 (in PAHs) or C sp3 in the hydrocarbon matrix in which PAHs were embedded in. Notably, CQDs had significant polar groups such as –NH– and –C–O–. These groups account for high solubility of CQDs in water.

Optical properties of N-CQDs are summarized in Fig. 3 . UV–Vis absorption spectrum, Fig. 3a, exhibits a shoulder at 238 nm, a broad peak centered at 347 nm, and an absorption tail extending over the visible region. The absorption tail, which is clearly seen in Fig. 3a for more concentrated N-CQDs solutions, is usually attributed to transitions from n-states of surface functional groups to π*-states of sp2 domains [10]. The shoulder at 238 nm can be assigned to π→π* transition of the C=C bonds [24], while the absorption band peaking at 347 nm is attributed to the transition within surface molecular-like fluorophore, e.g. 5-oxo-1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo-[1,2-α]pyridine-77-carboxylic acid (IPCA) [6,10]. Excitation-dependent PL spectra of N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3c. The emission intensity depended largely on the excitation wavelength which varied from 260 nm to 440 nm in this study as shown in Fig. 3c. The position of PL maximum intensity slightly changed from 477 nm to 498 nm as the excitation wavelength changed below 400 nm. When the excitation wavelength increased from 400 nm to 440 nm, the PL maximum position increased linearly from 489 nm to 517 nm. To obtain full excitation spectrum of CQDs, we measured PLE at 520 nm, which positioned well on the right side of PL spectrum, and the result is shown in Fig. 3b in comparison with the UV–Vis absorption spectrum. The PLE spectrum resembled the absorption spectrum above 260 nm, indicating that the emission originated directly from absorption excitation. The maximum PL emission yield was achieved at excitation of 355 nm. The relative emission quantum yield was estimated by comparing with quinine sulfate solution which exhibits well-known standard quantum yield of 55% as shown in Fig. 3d. The calculated quantum yield of N-CQDs was approximately 65.7%. The N-CQDs with a high emission quantum yield and water solubility would render attention for diverse applications such as PL probe and cell imaging. However, we observed that the PL intensity decreased gradually upon exposing to UV irradiation at 360 nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). We are investigating the origin of PL bleaching, and it will be reported in a separated paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of CQD-PVA composite
ce:section.ce:para: The UV instability of CQDs engages ones to embed CQDs into polymer matrix to retain the emission abilities of CQDs. Taking advantage of the similarity in solubility parameters of PVA and CQDs, herein, we make composite of CQDs and PVA, endowing fabrication of composite membranes by a simple bar coating method. The thickness of dried membranes was approximately 0.5 mm, and the concentration of CQDs in the composite was optimized to be about 5% by weight for optical studies.

The FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs, Fig. 4 a, exhibits stretching vibrations of polar groups (–OH, –NH–) at 3270 cm−1, stretching vibrations of aliphatic C-H at 2940 cm−1 and 2880 cm−1, and vibrational absorption of -CONH- at 1560 cm−1 and 1649 cm−1. When embedding N-CQDs into PVA matrix, two notable absorption peaks of (O–C=O) were observed at 844 cm−1 (C–O–C) at 1087 cm−1.

The absorption properties of composite (Comp) in solution and membrane states are summarized in Fig. 4b. As compared with N-CQDs or Comp in solution state, the absorption features of N-CQDs in solid composite state changed slightly, the absorption peak redshifted from 347 nm to 360 nm, and the absorption tail above 400 nm increased. The PL spectra of Comp with different excitation wavelengths were generally broad, ranging from 390 nm to 725 nm and did not have obvious PL maximum as seen in PL spectra of N-CQDs solution (Fig. 3c). In addition, PL (λex = 355 nm) was broader than that of N-CQDs solution (Supporting Information, Fig. S2). PLE spectra of Comp with observed wavelengths of 450 nm, 475 nm, 500 nm, and 520 nm were very similar and resembled the UV–vis absorption spectrum, as shown in Fig. 4c and Fig.S3 (Supporting Information). These features were akin to those of N-CQDs in solution state, indicating that N-CQDs response for the emission properties of Comp and that the emission from various centers giving rise to highest PL range (450 nm–520 nm) originates from the same excitation source. Significantly, in contrast to UV instability of N-CQDs (Supporting Information, Fig. S1), the optical properties of Comp maintained for over 60 h under UV irradiation, Fig. 4d. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 4e, embedding N-CQDs to PVA increases water resistance of Comp; this property can be further improved by adding CA as a crosslinking agent into the composite. CA also enhances thermal stability of Comp as indicated by TG curve shifting to higher temperature, Fig. 4f. It should be noted that comparing to CA, the water resistance and thermal stability were improved much more when Comp was crosslinked by K2S2O8 even though the emission was negligible.

Now, we will discuss the absorption redshift mentioned in Fig. 4b and the change in the emission spectra of N-CQDs in composite state. The dependences of absorption and emission positions on environment are well-established for organic dyes and have been recently studied on CQDs. Wang et al. studied the optical properties of CQDs derived from p-phenylenediamine dispersed in various solvents as well as polymers and pointed out a linear correlation between optical redshifts with the polarity of aprotic solvents or the –OH groups of protic solvents [25]. Pramanik et al. [26] and Mukherjee et al. [27] observed similar absorption redshifts of CQDs in protic solvents and attributed the shifts to the hydrogen bonding that stabilizes the excited states of surface functional groups, e.g. –NH, –OH, etc. Furthermore, in the cases that CQDs have emission centers originating from surface fluorophore and peripheral edge states, both of them also exhibit redshift induced by solvents having high hydrogen bonding capability [28]. Herein, adding PVA to N-CQDs solution did not change the absorption peak at 347 nm as seen in UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs (s) and Comp (s) in Fig. 4b. It means that water forms stronger hydrogen bonding with N-CQDs than PVA does. Therefore, the hydrogen bonding model alone could not explain the absorption redshift from solution state to membrane state as mentioned above. We supposed that in addition to hydrogen bonding between PVA and N-CQDs, the –OH groups of PVA could condense with surface functional groups of N-CQDs, such as –COOH groups. The bonding formation between CQDs and PVA matrix has been demonstrated for various types of CQDs [15–17,29] and is supported by the appearance of IR peak of (O–C=O) groups (Fig. 4a) and by the water-solubility reduction of Comp (Fig. 4e). As mentioned earlier, CQDs includes PAHs embedded in carbogenic core, surface fluorophore groups, and surface functional groups. The bonding with PVA matrix did not disturb PAHs because the absorption shoulder at 238 nm originating from π→π* transitions within PAHs did not change (Supporting Information, Fig. S4). In addition, because the absorption band maintained at 360 nm when the concentration of N-CQDs varied from 2.5% to 10%, the optical redshift was unlikely because of near field interactions between N-CQDs [30] (Supporting Information, Fig. S4). Likely, the redshift of absorption band, i.e. from 347 nm to 360 nm in Fig. 4b, is mainly due to the relaxing of quantum confinement of surface fluorophore groups followed by bonding formation with PVA via condensation processes. Similarly, bonding between PVA and surface functional groups of CQDs added new surface edge states [10], which account for the increased absorbance of the absorption tail 400 nm mentioned previously in Fig. 4b. Increased surface edge states also gave more recombination centers giving rise to enhanced PL intensity in low-energy region in Comp (Fig. 4c and Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photoluminescence quenching in composite by CA crosslinking
ce:section.ce:para: We demonstrated from Fig. 4 that embedding CQDs into PVA matrix enhanced the UV stability of CQDs and the water resistance of PVA. However, the resultant composite was still swollen and dissolved in water, inhibiting outdoor applications. Crosslinking PVA with CA was conducted to improve the water resistance of composites as shown in Fig. 4e. Interestingly, the composite with additional CA crosslinking (Comp + CA) had less intense PL as compared with composite without CA (Comp). To investigate the chemical origin of PL quenching, we first synthesized three types of CQDs with different surface chemistry by varying the –COOH/–NH2 ratio and then fabricated composite membranes with the same optical density at 360 nm. The FT-IR spectra of CQDs and the emission properties of Comp + CA are shown in Fig. 5 .

There were three notable absorption peaks in FT-IR spectra of CQDs, shown in Fig. 5a, located at 1710 cm−1, 1650 cm−1, and 1570 cm−1. The peaks at 1710 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 can be attributed to stretching of C=O in –COOH or –CONH– groups, respectively. The peak at 1570 cm−1 is due to bending vibration of N–H bond in –CONH–groups. Clearly, from A-CQDs to B-CQDs, the surface of CQDs turned from more acidic to more basic. The emission intensity of composite membranes was all decreased when adding CA as crosslinking agent as shown in Fig. 5b. Integrated PL decreased as much as 42% (for N-CQDs) or 43% (for B-CQDs), Fig. 5c. The PL loss was reduced to 19% by using A-CQDs. Likely, the trend of PL loss induced by CA crosslinking is in line with the surface basicity of CQDs.

PL quenching in CQD-polymer composites has been observed previously and debated to be due to CQD aggregation, which enhances energy transferring between CQDs via reabsorption [31] or creates new surface states aligning within the bandgap of CQDs [32]. Aggregation unlikely occurred in our cases because we did not observe scattering effects induced by aggregations in the UV–vis absorption spectra of CQD-PVA composites with increasing CQD contents (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). Surely, CA-crosslinking reduced the interdot spacing among CQDs as the composites became compacted. As a result, PL was partially quenched by a reabsorption mechanism which was deduced from the overlapping between the absorption and emission spectra of composite (Fig. 4c). The reabsorption mechanism was further supported by the fact that the emissions were quenched more significantly in the short-wavelength region (<450 nm) by CA-crosslinking, as seen in Fig. S5 and Table S1 (Supporting Information). However, reabsorption mechanism alone could not explain the dependence of PL quenching on the surface basicity of CQDs as discussed in Fig. 5b, and c.

Herein, we put forward a tentative mechanism, namely ‘crosslinking-induced tension’, as cartooned in Fig. 6 for A-CQDs and B-CQDs cases, to describe the effects of CQDs surfaces. We first adapted a structural model for CQDs, which include carbogenic PAHs, surface fluorophore, and surface polar groups [8,10,11]. FT-IR spectra of CQDs shown in Fig. 5a inferred that the surface of A-CQDs had more acidic groups, such as –COOH and citrazinic acid moiety while that of B-CQDs had more basic groups, e.g. –NH2, –CONH–, and –NH–. The carboxylic group of surfaces fluorophore presenting on A-CQDs surfaces could also convert into –CONH– or even –C–NH– by condensation reactions with excessive EDA in the case of B-CQDs. Therefore, in the presence of CA and B-CQDs easily reacted with CA to link to PVA matrix while A-CQDs remained unchanged. B-CQDs would experience a tension induced by matrix stress as CA crosslinked PVA chains. The external strain experienced by capping ligands was reported to displace QDs' surface atoms from their relaxed positions resulting new energy states within the QDs’ bandgap [33–35]. These states provide non-radiative recombination pathways to quench PL. We also observed the same phenomena in InP and InP/ZnS–organosilicon composites where the QDs bonded to silicone matrix via surface ligands [36]. Similarly, the tension induced by CA-crosslinking could quench the emission of both carbogenic core and surface fluorophore groups of CQDs in Comp + CA. Concretely, we assigned PL peaks at 430 nm and 510 nm, respectively, to the emissions from carbogenic core and surface fluorophore groups [11]. Comparing PL intensities at these wavelengths between Comp and Comp + CA (Table S1, Supporting Information) revealed that CA-crosslinking reduced the emission of surface fluorophore (carbogenic core) by 6% (23%) in the case of A-CQDs and by 39% (44%) in the case of B-CQDs. The quenching efficiencies were higher in Comp + CA of B-CQDs because amine groups presenting both in the carbogenic core and the surface fluorophore groups readily reacted with CA crosslinker. The direct chemical bonds between B-CQDs and PVA matrix delivered a higher external tension as PVA chains were crosslinked by CA. Whereas, A-CQDs with more acidic groups, which did not react with CA, would experience lesser matrix stress. The PL quenching in the case of A-CQDs arose mostly from decreasing emission of carbogenic core at 430 nm, which was probably due to the reabsorption mechanism mentioned previously. Alternatively, as compared with A-CQDs, complex reactions between B-CQDs and CA in Comp + CA resulted in more surface states for non-radiative recombination, which reduced PL more efficiently.

Highly luminescent CQDs were hydrothermally synthesized from CA and EDA for fabrication of CQD-PVA composite membranes. Hydrogen bonding formation as well as condensation reaction between CQDs and PVA increased the water resistant of composites as compared with PVA alone. The chemical bonding between CQDs and PVA matrix also induced redshift of the absorption band originating from surface fluorophore of CQDs. Crosslinking CQD-PVA composites with additional CA enhanced the water resistance and the thermal stability, but it reduced the photoluminescence efficiency as much as 40%. Study the PL loss on CQDs differentiating in surface chemistry revealed that CA-crosslinking affected lesser on CQDs bearing acidic surfaces. CA-crosslinking quenched the PL of composites by increased reabsorption and induced tensions to CQDs via chemical bonding. The results demonstrated here in point out the importance of chemical interaction between CQDs polymer matrix on the optical properties of composites.
The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hanoi Pedagogical University 2 (HPU2) under Grant no. C.2017-18-05 and partially funded by Vietnam Ministry of Education and Training via the Research and Development Foundation of HPU2 under Grant no B.2018-SP2-13.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2019.01.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent sing-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Solvent-controlled synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots with a wide color gamut and narrowed emission peak widths
Engineering triangular carbon quantum dots with unprecedented narrow bandwidth emission for multicolored LEDs
Synthesis of luminescent carbon dots with ultrahigh quantum yield and inherent folate receptor-positive cancer cell targetability
Carbon quantum dots for zebrafish fluorescence imaging

Tracking the source of carbon dot photoluminescence: aromatic domains versus molecular fluorophores
Luminescence origin of carbon based dots obtained from citric acid and amino group-containing molecules
Molecular fluorescence in citric acid-based carbon dots
Molecular origin and self-assembly of fluorescent carbon nanodots in polar solvents
Resolving the multiple emission centers in carbon dots: from fluorophore molecular states to aromatic domain states and carbon-core states
Newly synthesized silicon quantum dot–polystyrene nanocomposite having thermally robust positive charge trapping
Enhanced red emission in ultrasound-assisted sol-gel derived ZnO/PMMA nanocomposite
The large-scale synthesis of vinyl-functionalized silicon quantum dot and its application in miniemulsion polymerization
Refractive-index tuning of highly fluorescent carbon dots
Large-scale one-step synthesis of carbon dots from yeast extract powder and construction of carbon dots/PVA fluorescent shape memory material
Luminescent poly(vinyl alcohol)/carbon quantum dots composites with tunable water-induced shape memory behavior in different pH and temperature environments
Study of electrical charge storage in polymer-carbon quantum dot composite
A facile and low-cost method to enhance the internal quantum yield and external light-extraction efficiency for flexible light-emitting carbon-dot films
Poly(ethylene glycol)/carbon quantum dot composite solid films exhibiting intense and tunable blue–red emission
Freestanding luminescent films of nitrogen-rich carbon nanodots toward large-scale phosphor-based white-light-emitting devices
New crosslinked cast films based on poly(vinyl alcohol): preparation and physico-chemical properties
Artifacts and errors associated with the ubiquitous presence of fluorescent impurities in carbon nanodots
Investigation of structural and electronic properties of graphene oxide
Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots
